name: Money — Coins, Notes, and Making Amounts
whatIsIt: Money comes in COINS and NOTES of set values: ₹1, ₹2, ₹5, ₹10, ₹20, ₹50, ₹100. We can MAKE an amount in different ways (₹10 as a ten-note, or two fives, or ten ones), ADD up what we have, and work out CHANGE — what's left after we spend.
whyNeeded: Money is maths children see every day. Knowing coin and note values, counting a handful, making an exact amount, and checking change are real-life skills that make counting, place value and addition matter.
realLifeUses: Counting the coins in a piggy bank
Paying the exact amount for a snack
Checking the change after buying something
Saving up ₹50 in fives and tens

vocabulary.meaning: Money pieces of set value: ₹1, ₹2, ₹5 coins; ₹10, ₹20, ₹50, ₹100 notes.
The total value of the money you have.
Choosing coins/notes that add up to a value (₹10 = 5 + 5).
The money you get back after paying more than the price.

story.title: Buying a Clay Horse at the Fair
story.text: At the village fair, Ravi wants a ₹15 clay horse. He has a ₹10 note and a ₹5 coin. 'Do I have enough?' The stall seller nods: '10 and 5 make exactly 15 — perfect!' Later a balloon costs ₹8 and Ravi pays with a ₹10 note. 'Your change?' 'Ten take away eight... 2 rupees back!' Ravi grins. 'Money is just counting and adding — with rupees!'
story.extractedProblem: Ravi pays for a ₹15 clay horse with a ₹10 note and a ₹5 coin. Does he have exactly enough?

visual.caption: ₹10 and ₹5 make ₹15. Add up coins and notes to find the amount.

teachingGallery.title: Coins and notes — making amounts
teachingGallery.note: Count coins and notes to make the money you need.

examples.caption: One ₹10 note = the same as ten ₹1 coins.

standardMethod.summary: Add up the values of the coins and notes; to make an amount, pick pieces that total it; change = paid − price.
steps: To find how much you have, ADD the values (biggest first helps).
To MAKE an amount, choose pieces that add to exactly that value.

alternateMethods.name: Biggest-first counting
alternateMethods.whenToUse: To count a handful of money.

alternateMethods.name: Count-up for change
steps: Count up to the amount paid.
How much you counted is the change.

workedExamples.problem: How much is a ₹10 note and two ₹2 coins together?
You buy a ₹6 pencil with a ₹10 note. What change do you get?

commonMistakes.mistake: Counting the NUMBER of coins instead of their value (calling three ₹5 coins '₹3').
commonMistakes.fix: Add the VALUES, not the count. Three ₹5 coins = 5 + 5 + 5 = ₹15, not ₹3.

commonMistakes.mistake: Adding the price and the money paid to find change instead of subtracting.
commonMistakes.fix: Change is what's LEFT: money paid − price. Paid ₹10 for a ₹6 item → 10 − 6 = ₹4 change, not 16.

commonMistakes.mistake: Mixing up note values, like thinking a ₹20 note is ₹2.
commonMistakes.fix: Read the number on the note carefully: ₹20 is twenty rupees, ten times a ₹2 coin. Know the set: 1, 2, 5, 10, 20, 50, 100.

easy.q: A ₹10 note and a ₹5 coin — how many rupees together?
Two ₹5 coins make how many rupees?
hintLadder: Add the values, not the count.

medium.q: A ₹20 note and a ₹10 note = how many rupees?
You pay ₹10 for a ₹7 snack. What change (in rupees)?

challenge.q: You pay a ₹50 note for a ₹35 book. Change in rupees?

questions.q: A ₹5 coin and two ₹2 coins = ? rupees
Pay ₹20 for a ₹14 toy. Change in rupees?

teachBackPrompt: Use real or paper coins and notes. Teach someone at home to MAKE ₹20 in two different ways, count a small handful by adding values (not counting coins), and work out change from ₹10.

revisionCard.summary: Money values: ₹1,2,5,10,20,50,100. Add the VALUES (not the count) to find an amount. Make an amount by choosing pieces that total it. Change = paid − price.

gameMission.title: Fun at the Fair
gameMission.brief: Count coins and notes, make exact amounts, and give the right change at the fair stalls.

realLifeProject: With a grown-up's play money or coins, make ₹25 in two different ways. Then 'buy' something and work out the change.

rememberIt.hook: 100 paise = 1 rupee. Count big coins first
rememberIt.unpack: Count notes and big coins first, then the small ones. To give change, count UP from the price to the amount paid.

studentQuestions.q: How do I work out change?
studentQuestions.a: Count UP from the price to what you paid. Bought for 30, gave 50: 30 -> 40 -> 50 is 20 change.

studentQuestions.q: Which coins do I count first?
studentQuestions.a: Biggest first (notes, then big coins), then the small ones - faster, and you will not lose track.
